answer:
In "Aaftab" (ch. 14), Iqbal directly apostrophizes the sun (*Aaftab*) as a divine, eternal force—the "soul" (*rooh-o-rawaan*) of the cosmos that binds existence, imparts life and consciousness (*shaoor*), and transcends time.[^1] It is personified as heart, intellect (*khirad*), and radiant awareness illuminating all being, with the poet imploring it to bestow enlightenment upon humanity.[^2]

By contrast, in "Mehr-e-Roshan" (ch. 32), the "shining sun" (*mehr-e-roshan*) marks a moment of poignant departure at dusk, its veiled setting (*chhip gaya*) draping the world in mourning and unveiling night's mysteries.[^3] This triggers an elegiac inquiry to the dead ("sleepers in dust"), using the sun's absence as a metaphor for mortality and transition, prompting existential questions about the afterlife rather than celebrating cosmic vitality.[^4]

**Key difference:** "Aaftab" elevates the sun as an active, life-affirming symbol of divine consciousness and unity; "Mehr-e-Roshan" deploys it transiently as a harbinger of loss, solitude, and the veil between worlds.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 14 "Aaftab" (translation), [p-96b3da](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/#p-96b3da), [p-0d24a0](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/#p-0d24a0).

[^2]: *Ibid.*, [p-fdcce3](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/#p-fdcce3), [p-af377c](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/#p-af377c).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 32 "Mehr-e-roshan" (translation), [p-202a06](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/32-mehr-e-roshan/translation/#p-202a06).

[^4]: *Ibid.*, [p-fab1a6](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/32-mehr-e-roshan/translation/#p-fab1a6), [p-9e70bf](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/32-mehr-e-roshan/translation/#p-9e70bf).
